title: Sourdough discard banana bread
subtitle: Quick loaf using unfed sourdough starter to add depth and a gentle tang.
excerpt: Sourdough discard banana bread uses the portion of starter you would otherwise tip away. The discard adds a mild acidity that offsets the sweetness of very ripe bananas, and it contributes to the open, moist crumb. This is a quick bread: no proving, no kneading. Mix, pour, bake for an hour.
sourceNotes: Banana bread became popular in the United States during the 1930s when bicarbonate of soda and baking powder became widely available as commercial leaveners. This version adds sourdough discard, a practice that grew alongside the revival of home sourdough baking from the early 2000s onwards. The discard acts as an additional flavour contributor rather than as the primary leavener; bicarbonate of soda does the primary rise. Standard quick-bread ratios from American baking tradition underpin the proportions.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice the cooled loaf and freeze flat in a bag. Defrost at room temperature for an hour, or toast slices from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The loaf keeps well. Store in an airtight tin at room temperature for three days. The flavour is better on day two.

recipeTools.notes: Standard 2 lb (900 g) loaf tin, greased and lined.

glossaryTerms.definition: The portion of sourdough starter removed before a feeding. It contains wild yeast and lactic acid bacteria but is not at peak activity, so it cannot leaven bread on its own. Used in quick breads, pancakes, and crackers as a flavour addition rather than a primary leavener.

content.text: Sourdough discard sits in the fridge between feedings, slightly acidic and full of spent wild yeast. Most recipes treat it as waste. This banana bread uses it as an ingredient: the tang cuts the sweetness of overripe bananas and adds a layer that a plain quick bread lacks.

does not leaven the bread; bicarbonate of soda does that. The discard is there for flavour. Use discard that is up to a week old straight from the fridge. Very old, very acidic discard will make the bread taste vinegary rather than tangy.

items.prepNote: very ripe, black-spotted, about 300 g mashed

content.text: Heat the oven to 170°C fan (190°C conventional). Grease and line a standard 2 lb (900 g) loaf tin with baking paper.
Peel {{banana}} and mash in a large bowl until almost smooth; a few lumps are fine. Stir in {{sourdough-starter}}, {{unsalted-butter}}, {{eggs}}, and {{vanilla-extract}} until combined.
In a separate bowl, mix {{plain-flour}} and {{light-brown-sugar}}. Add {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} and whisk to combine.
Fold the dry ingredients into the wet mixture with a spatula. Stop as soon as no dry flour shows; a few streaks are acceptable. Stir through the {{walnuts}} if using. Overmixing makes the loaf tough.

Pour into the tin and level the top. Bake for 55 to 65 minutes. The loaf is done when a skewer inserted in the centre comes out with a few moist crumbs but no wet batter, and the top is deeply browned. If the top is browning too fast after 40 minutes, lay a sheet of foil loosely over it.
Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a rack. Leave for at least 20 minutes before slicing; hot banana bread crumbles.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Quick breads are simple but have a few predictable failure points.

items.symptom: Loaf sank in the middle after cooling.
items.cause: Underbaked, or the tin was moved while baking.
items.fix: Test with a skewer: the centre must read clean before taking it out. Do not open the oven door in the first 30 minutes.

items.symptom: Loaf is dense and gummy throughout.
items.cause: Too much banana, or overmixed batter.
items.fix: Weigh the mashed banana; a soft banana loses weight. Three medium-sized bananas should yield about 280 to 300 g mashed. Mix until just combined.

items.symptom: Top browned but centre still wet at 60 minutes.
items.cause: Oven running hot, or tin too small.
items.fix: Cover with foil and continue baking in 10-minute increments. A cold banana or cold eggs slow down the bake at the start.
